column46:
There are three parts to the Town Hall meeting. 

The first part was the Civic Engagement and it was not well run/managed. There were 400 people told to roam around in a small area and find a booth that fit our policy topic. There was no booth that I saw that really fit my topic so I asked to help desk. They told me what booth to go to, but because it was not well run/managed there was just a mob of people around this one booth run by one girl and when I asked about my policy topic she said it was not in her realm of expertise and she could not speak to it. So on my sheet, I just had to write down N/A.

The second part was the Break Out Rooms where we met with people of like topic. I finished at the Civic Engagement early apparently because I was the first one in the room by about 15 minutes. Then when everyone got there and began to talk about their more narrow topic we discovered that all of our topics for the most part were different. So the first break out was the whole class and for the most part, it was one student and the "expert" talking/arguing back and forth. The second breakout did not go much better. The mediator and the experts were supposed to bounce around and make sure the groups stayed on topic but no one really talked to my group and no one in my group would say anything of substance. They would make generalized statements that no one can disagree with like "human trafficking and drugs are bad so we need more border security". That's great but then no one would offer up how to increase border security in a good way and anytime I tried the topic would change to another broad sweeping statement.

The final part was trying to get students aware of how they could enact change in the community. This was done fairly well, I don't really know how I would have done it differently so that was probably the best part of the night for me. It was somewhat useful information and since the groups were split between Colusa and the Library then I was actually able to have a conversation and learn something.

The instructions were pretty unclear and I was so stressed that I wasn't being entirely present in my conversations with these groups. I felt that this stress was mutual as well and everyone was in a rush. When we went into discussion groups, I did feel that the consultants provided a lot of insight, and we had a productive conversation in which everyone contributed. Finally, we finished out by discovering ways to contact government officials or attract their attention in some way, which I found to be organized more efficiently and helpful.

column46: I would describe it as an event to meet people that have some of the same ideas as you. It was fun, but definitely could be more organized and less stressful. I met a lot of new people/friends which I loved the most about it. Some of the rooms and spaces were super small to hold a lot of the people there, but I do understand just because of the limited space available. If I had a choice to go, I probably would not as I didn’t feel confident in my work prior to it, but I did like going so it could help my grade in the long run. One reason why I did not feel confident is because I had filled out some sections incorrectly and didn’t have a lot of time to get it worked on. This was on me because I was busy personally, but I felt like I wasn’t going to be prepared during it. Thankfully, we didn’t go too deep into it and I could talk about what I had already.

column46: If I am going to be brutally honest, this was one of the worst ran events I have attended. The beginning of the event forced people to wander around the cramped spaces of the library like headless chickens. Only having small signs to say what organization was on the table proved to be completely ineffective since everyone was walking around. Nobody knew where they were going unless they somehow stumbled upon a table that may or may not have been related to their policy area. The small groups were alright, but they ran out of cookies for our section which is very minor, but disappointing nonetheless. Once we got back to the library, it was just as disorganized and chaotic as it was during the start. The way the tables were set up made it so it was hard to actually sit down and listen to what people had to say, people who were volunteering their time at that. It beats me why this wasn't held at Colusa hall or the BMU where it could have been much more organized with more room to work with. Overall, this proved to be a very disappointing result with all of the work that led up to it.
